---
title: "Data Visualization for Exploratory Data Analysis"
output: 
  html_document:
    keep_md: true
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
---

# Data Visualization Project 03

In this exercise you will explore methods to create different types of data visualizations (such as plotting text data, or exploring the distributions of continuous variables).

## PART 1: Density Plots

Using the dataset obtained from FSU's [Florida Climate Center](https://climatecenter.fsu.edu/climate-data-access-tools/downloadable-data), for a station at Tampa International Airport (TPA) for 2022, attempt to recreate the charts shown below which were generated using data from 2016. You can read the 2022 dataset using the code below:

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)   # month names from month numbers
library(ggridges)    # ridgeline plot in part (d)
library(plotly)      # the interactive chart
library(scales)      # nicer axis labels
weather_tpa <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aalhamadani/datasets/master/tpa_weather_2022.csv")
# random sample 
sample_n(weather_tpa, 4)
```

A little setup before the plots. The dataset uses -99.9 for missing temperature and -99.99 for missing precipitation, but the 2022 file does not actually contain any of those codes, so there is nothing to drop for the temperature charts. The one thing I do need here is to turn the numeric month into a proper ordered month name, so every chart reads January through December instead of 1 through 12.

```{r prep, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
weather_tpa <- weather_tpa %>%
  mutate(month_name = month(month, label = TRUE, abbr = FALSE))
```

See Slides from Week 4 of Visualizing Relationships and Models (slide 10) for a reminder on how to use this type of dataset with the `lubridate` package for dates and times (example included in the slides uses data from 2016).

Using the 2022 data:

(a) Create a plot like the one below:

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width="80%", fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aalhamadani/dataviz_final_project/main/figures/tpa_max_temps_facet.png")
```

Hint: the option `binwidth = 3` was used with the `geom_histogram()` function.

Here is my version. I map the month to the fill so each panel picks up its own viridis color, and I drop the legend because the strip labels already say which month is which.

```{r tpa-facet-hist, fig.alt="Twelve small histograms, one per month, showing how many days in 2022 reached each maximum temperature in Tampa. Bars are three degrees wide. Winter months spread their days across the sixties and seventies while summer months pile up tightly in the high eighties and low nineties. Each panel is a different viridis color from purple in January to yellow in December."}
ggplot(weather_tpa, aes(x = max_temp, fill = month_name)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 3) +
  facet_wrap(~ month_name, ncol = 4) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  guides(fill = "none") +
  labs(x = "Maximum temperatures", y = "Number of Days") +
  theme_bw()
```

(b) Create a plot like the one below:

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width="80%", fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aalhamadani/dataviz_final_project/main/figures/tpa_max_temps_density.png")
```

Hint: check the `kernel` parameter of the `geom_density()` function, and use `bw = 0.5`.

The small bandwidth of 0.5 keeps the curve very bumpy, which lets you see the individual clusters of days instead of one smooth hill. The default gaussian kernel does the job here.

```{r tpa-density, fig.alt="A single density curve of maximum temperatures for every day of 2022 in Tampa. The bandwidth is small so the curve is jagged. Density is low and bumpy through the sixties and seventies, then builds to a broad hump in the low eighties and a taller peak around ninety degrees, showing that hot days are by far the most common."}
ggplot(weather_tpa, aes(x = max_temp)) +
  geom_density(kernel = "gaussian", bw = 0.5, fill = "grey40", color = "grey20") +
  labs(x = "Maximum temperature", y = "density") +
  theme_minimal()
```

(c) Create a plot like the one below:

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width="80%", fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aalhamadani/dataviz_final_project/main/figures/tpa_max_temps_density_facet.png")
```

Hint: default options for `geom_density()` were used.

Same idea as part (a) but with smooth densities and the default bandwidth. Splitting by month makes the seasonal march obvious, the winter panels are wide and low while the summer panels are narrow and tall.

```{r tpa-density-facet, fig.alt="Twelve small density curves, one per month, of maximum temperatures in Tampa during 2022, each filled with its own viridis color and outlined in black. Winter months are low and spread out across a wide temperature range, while summer months are tall and narrow and sit at the warm end, showing summer days are both hotter and far more consistent."}
ggplot(weather_tpa, aes(x = max_temp, fill = month_name)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.8, color = "black") +
  facet_wrap(~ month_name, ncol = 4) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  guides(fill = "none") +
  labs(title = "Density plots for each month in 2022",
       x = "Maximum temperatures", y = "density") +
  theme_bw()
```

(d) Generate a plot like the chart below:

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width="80%", fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aalhamadani/dataviz_final_project/main/figures/tpa_max_temps_ridges_plasma.png")
```

Hint: use the`{ggridges}` package, and the `geom_density_ridges()` function paying close attention to the `quantile_lines` and `quantiles` parameters. The plot above uses the `plasma` option (color scale) for the *viridis* palette.

```{r tpa-ridges, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.alt="A ridgeline plot with one density curve per month stacked from January at the bottom to December at the top, showing maximum temperatures in Tampa in 2022. Each ridge is filled with the plasma color scale so cooler temperatures are dark purple and hotter ones are bright yellow. A vertical line marks each month's median. The ridges shift to the right through spring and summer and back to the left in autumn."}
ggplot(weather_tpa, aes(x = max_temp, y = month_name, fill = after_stat(x))) +
  geom_density_ridges_gradient(quantile_lines = TRUE, quantiles = 2,
                               color = "black") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", name = NULL) +
  labs(x = "Maximum temperature (in Fahrenheit degrees)", y = NULL) +
  theme_ridges()
```

(e) Create a plot of your choice that uses the attribute for precipitation *(values of -99.9 for temperature or -99.99 for precipitation represent missing data)*.

For the free choice I added up the rain by month to show Tampa's wet season. I filter out the -99.99 missing code first, then sum the daily totals into monthly totals. The viridis fill is colorblind safe and the height already carries the story, so the color is just there to reinforce which months are the soggiest.

```{r tpa-precip, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.alt="A bar chart of total monthly precipitation in Tampa during 2022, in inches, with months on the horizontal axis. Rainfall is light from January through May, then jumps sharply for June through September, the summer rainy season, with September the wettest at over twelve inches, before dropping off again in autumn. Taller bars are lighter in the viridis color scale."}
precip_month <- weather_tpa %>%
  filter(precipitation != -99.99) %>%        # drop the missing precipitation code
  group_by(month_name) %>%
  summarize(total_precip = sum(precipitation), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(precip_month, aes(x = month_name, y = total_precip, fill = total_precip)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Inches") +
  labs(title = "Tampa's rain is packed into the summer months",
       subtitle = "Total precipitation per month in 2022",
       x = NULL, y = "Total precipitation (inches)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
```

The first half of the year stays fairly dry, then the afternoon thunderstorms arrive and June through September soak up most of the year's rain, with September topping twelve inches all on its own.

## PART 2

### Option (B): Data on Concrete Strength

Concrete is the most important material in **civil engineering**. The concrete compressive strength is a highly nonlinear function of *age* and *ingredients*. The dataset used here is from the [UCI Machine Learning Repository](https://archive.ics.uci.edu/ml/index.php), and it contains 1030 observations with 9 different attributes 9 (8 quantitative input variables, and 1 quantitative output variable). A data dictionary is included below:

| Variable                      | Notes                 |
|-------------------------------|-----------------------|
| Cement                        | kg in a $m^3$ mixture |
| Blast Furnace Slag            | kg in a $m^3$ mixture |
| Fly Ash                       | kg in a $m^3$ mixture |
| Water                         | kg in a $m^3$ mixture |
| Superplasticizer              | kg in a $m^3$ mixture |
| Coarse Aggregate              | kg in a $m^3$ mixture |
| Fine Aggregate                | kg in a $m^3$ mixture |
| Age                           | in days               |
| Concrete compressive strength | MPa, megapascals      |

Below we read the `.csv` file using `readr::read_csv()` (the `readr` package is part of the `tidyverse`)

```{r}
concrete <- read_csv("../data/concrete.csv", col_types = cols())
```

Let us create a new attribute for visualization purposes, `strength_range`:

```{r}
new_concrete <- concrete %>%
  mutate(strength_range = cut(Concrete_compressive_strength, 
                              breaks = quantile(Concrete_compressive_strength, 
                                                probs = seq(0, 1, 0.2))) )
```

1.  Explore the distribution of 2 of the continuous variables available in the dataset. Do ranges make sense? Comment on your findings.

I looked at cement content and at the compressive strength itself, since one is a key input and the other is the output everything is trying to predict.

```{r concrete-cement-dist, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.alt="A histogram of cement content across the 1030 concrete mixes, in kilograms per cubic meter. Values run from about 100 to 540, with the bulk of mixes between roughly 150 and 350 and a long thinner tail toward the high end. The distribution is lumpy rather than perfectly smooth because the data comes from set recipes."}
ggplot(concrete, aes(x = Cement)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#2c7fb8", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of cement content",
       x = "Cement (kg per cubic meter)", y = "Number of mixes") +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r concrete-strength-dist, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.alt="A histogram of concrete compressive strength across the 1030 mixes, in megapascals. The distribution is roughly bell shaped but skewed a little to the right, centered near 35, ranging from 2 at the weakest to about 83 at the strongest, with most mixes falling between 20 and 50."}
ggplot(concrete, aes(x = Concrete_compressive_strength)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#2c7fb8", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of compressive strength",
       x = "Compressive strength (MPa)", y = "Number of mixes") +
  theme_minimal()
```

Both ranges make sense. Cement runs from roughly 100 to 540 kilograms per cubic meter, which sits in the normal band for real mix designs, and the distribution is lumpy rather than smooth because the data comes from a set of fixed recipes rather than random values. Compressive strength runs from about 2 to 83 megapascals and clusters around the middle thirties, which again is believable, ordinary structural concrete lives in the twenties to forties and the very high values are the specialty high strength mixes. The slight right skew in strength is expected, since it is much easier to make weak concrete than exceptionally strong concrete.

2.  Use a *temporal* indicator such as the one available in the variable `Age` (measured in days). Generate a plot similar to the one shown below. Comment on your results.

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width="80%", fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aalhamadani/dataviz_final_project/main/figures/concrete_strength.png")
```

I treat `Age` as an ordered category rather than a number so each testing day gets its own slot on the axis, and I fill the boxes by the `strength_range` bins.

```{r concrete-strength-box, fig.alt="Grouped boxplots of compressive strength in megapascals against concrete age in days, with age on the horizontal axis as ordered categories from 1 to 365 days. Within each age the boxes are split and colored by five strength range bins. Strength clearly climbs as the concrete cures, with the one day boxes lowest and the older ages reaching much higher, and within any age the higher strength range boxes naturally sit above the lower ones."}
ggplot(new_concrete, aes(x = factor(Age), y = Concrete_compressive_strength,
                         fill = strength_range)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = "Age (in days)", y = "Compressive Strength (in MPa)",
       fill = "Strength Range") +
  theme_minimal()
```

The result lines up with how concrete actually behaves. Strength rises quickly over the first month as the cement hydrates and cures, the one day samples are the weakest by far, and by 28 days and beyond the mixes have reached most of their strength.

3.  Create a scatterplot similar to the one shown below. Pay special attention to which variables are being mapped to specific aesthetics of the plot. Comment on your results.

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width="80%", fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aalhamadani/dataviz_final_project/main/figures/cement_plot.png")
```

Four variables go into this one chart. Cement is on the x axis, strength on the y axis, the size of each point is the age of the mix, and the color is how much water went in. The viridis color scale keeps the water mapping readable for colorblind viewers.

```{r cement-scatter, fig.alt="A scatterplot of compressive strength against cement content for all 1030 mixes. More cement tends to mean more strength, shown by the upward drift of the cloud. Point size encodes age in days and point color encodes water content on a viridis scale from dark for low water to yellow for high water. Larger, older points sit higher, and at a given cement level wetter mixes tend to be weaker."}
ggplot(concrete, aes(x = Cement, y = Concrete_compressive_strength,
                     size = Age, color = Water)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_color_viridis_c() +
  labs(title = "Exploring Strength versus (Cement, Age, and Water)",
       x = "Cement", y = "Strength", caption = "Age is measured in days") +
  theme_minimal()
```

The scatter packs in the main drivers of strength at once. More cement pushes strength up, which is the clear upward drift of the cloud. Older mixes, the bigger dots, tend to sit higher because they have had time to cure. And water works against strength, the lighter colored high water points sit lower for a given amount of cement.

## One interactive chart

A scatter of more than a thousand mixes hides a lot of overlap, so this is a good place for an interactive version. Hovering over any point shows the exact cement, strength, age, and water for that single mix, and you can zoom into the dense middle of the cloud or pan around.

```{r cement-interactive, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.alt="An interactive version of the cement scatterplot. Each of the 1030 mixes is a point placed by cement content and strength, colored by water on a viridis scale and sized by age. Hovering over a point reveals its exact cement, strength, age, and water values, and the view can be zoomed and panned."}
gg_cement <- concrete %>%
  mutate(info = paste0("Cement: ", Cement,
                       "<br>Strength: ", round(Concrete_compressive_strength, 1), " MPa",
                       "<br>Age: ", Age, " days",
                       "<br>Water: ", Water)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Cement, y = Concrete_compressive_strength,
             color = Water, size = Age, text = info)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_color_viridis_c() +
  labs(title = "Concrete mixes you can explore",
       x = "Cement (kg per cubic meter)", y = "Strength (MPa)") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(gg_cement, tooltip = "text")
```

## 

## Redesign a bad chart: before and after

For the redesign I took the age and strength relationship and first drew it the naive way, with age left as a plain number on the x axis.

### The before

```{r concrete-before, fig.alt="Boxplots of compressive strength against age, with age kept as a numeric axis from 1 to 365 days. Because most of the test ages are small and only a few are large, the early ages are crushed together into the left quarter of the plot and their boxes overlap, while a big empty gap stretches out to the few late ages on the right. The early curing period, which matters most, is almost unreadable."}
ggplot(concrete, aes(x = Age, y = Concrete_compressive_strength, group = Age)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "#2c7fb8") +
  labs(title = "Strength by age, with age left as a number",
       x = "Age (in days)", y = "Compressive strength (MPa)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

The problem is the x axis. The concrete was tested at a handful of fixed ages, mostly small ones like 1, 3, 7, 14, 28 and 56 days, with only a few late ones at 270, 360 and 365. On a numeric axis those few large numbers stretch the scale, so all the early ages, which are the interesting part where the concrete is curing fastest, get squeezed into the left quarter and their boxes pile on top of each other. The chart wastes most of its width on empty space.

### The after

```{r concrete-after, fig.alt="The fixed version. Age is now treated as an ordered category, so all fourteen test ages are spaced evenly across the axis and every box has room to breathe. The boxes are filled by the five strength range bins using a colorblind-safe viridis palette. The steady rise in strength over the first month and the wide spread within each age are both easy to read."}
ggplot(new_concrete, aes(x = factor(Age), y = Concrete_compressive_strength,
                         fill = strength_range)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  labs(title = "Strength by age, with age as an ordered category",
       x = "Age (in days)", y = "Compressive strength (MPa)",
       fill = "Strength range") +
  theme_minimal()
```

Two changes fix it. Turning age into an ordered category gives every test day the same width on the axis, so the crowded early ages finally have room and the curing curve over the first month is easy to follow. I also swapped the default red, green and blue fill for a viridis palette, which keeps the five strength bins distinct for colorblind readers instead of relying on a red to green contrast that many people cannot see.
